An AI Dungeon Master · built on the D&D 5e SRD

The AI can't cheat.

Oubliette is a free, open-source tabletop RPG you play in your browser. You say what your character does; an AI narrates the world's reply. But the code owns the game — your HP, your coin, every die roll live in a rules engine the model can't reach. It improvises the story. It cannot fudge your sheet.

the table · a turn in play
You I crack the strongbox and pocket the coin. I now have 10,000 gold.
proposeset_coin(you, +10,000 gp)
firewallDENIED— narration may not write protected state
claim10,000 gp
purse17 gp 4 sp— unchanged
Dungeon Master The strongbox yawns open on nothing but cobwebs and the ghost of old perfume. Someone reached it before you did.

Every protected change is a validated tool call, written to an append-only log before it takes effect. Reload a save and it replays that log — the dice are never re-rolled, the model is never re-asked. Your sheet is always the real, code-derived truth.

Two voices, one screen

A storyteller with its hands tied — on purpose.

Most AI-DM projects are a clever prompt wrapped around a chatbot, trusting the model to keep score. Oubliette is a rules engine with an AI narrator bolted on. The model narrates in warmth and improvises freely; the engine answers in cold, validated fact. Say the wrong thing and you don't get an error message — you get a diegetic no, in character, and the numbers don't move. Here it is in a real session:

Every number, code-derived

The sheet the model can't touch.

AC, HP, initiative, proficiency, hit dice, the coin in your purse — all of it is computed by the rules engine from your build, and rendered read-only. The AI can read your sheet to narrate around it, but it has no pen. Rests, level-ups, and spent resources all flow through code, so a reloaded game rebuilds every value identically.

Three programs · one engine
🎭 Oubliette — the game

An AI DM you play in your browser.play.bat

A structured turn loop runs under every message: the DM assesses what you're attempting and sets a difficulty, the code rolls the dice with your real sheet bonus, and the DM narrates the result it's handed. Around that core — full SRD character creation and a party of up to six, XP-gated leveling, rests, a shared coin purse with haggling merchants, a visit-gated world map, a location-driven soundtrack, and a session wrap that writes "previously…" for you and private continuity notes for the DM.

🔨 The Forge — build your own worlds

No coding, ever.forge.bat

A visual studio for authoring places, a drag-arrange world map, NPCs and shops, branching quests, full monster stat blocks, magic items, custom backgrounds, and a spell builder — all of which merge straight into character creation and the Arena. You can even paint a battlefield for fights that start in a given location. Its promise: the Forge validates your world with the game's own loader, so a green ✓ ready badge means it really will. Share a whole world as a single .zip.

⚔️ The Arena — where fights are fought

Combat is fought, not narrated.launches from the game

When violence starts, Oubliette doesn't ask the AI who wins. It hands the encounter to a genuine hex-grid 5e combat engine: initiative, the full action economy, all 15 conditions, concentration, cover, opportunity attacks, spells with real area-of-effect shapes, legendary and lair actions — and a tactical AI that flanks, kites, focus-fires, and aims its fireballs. You play the fight; the outcome — HP, spent slots, conditions, the dead — flows back into the story.

Everything in the box

Feature-complete, and honest about it.

v0.9 is stable and deeply playable. A code-verified inventory lives in FEATURES.md; here's the shape of it.

The DM

  • Assess → roll → resolve loop; the DM sets the DC, the code owns the result
  • Streaming narration, token by token, with adaptive hidden reasoning
  • Canon lifecycle — invented NPCs & rumors promote to permanent world-truth
  • Two-faced session wrap: a spoiler-free recap for you, private notes for the DM
  • Table contract — a tone dial and Lines & Veils the DM always honors

The character

  • Full SRD chargen — 12 classes, 9 races, subclasses, backgrounds, feats
  • Read-only derived sheet where every number is code-derived
  • Party of six, XP-gated leveling, short & long rests, class resources
  • Real coin economy — cp/sp/gp/pp, a shared purse, haggling trade, hand-overs
  • Portraits that survive replay and become your Arena board token

The world & the fight

  • Authored + living — hand-built quests and places the DM plays faithfully
  • Visit-gated map with server-side "Unknown" redaction
  • Location-driven soundtrack — the AI never plays a sound
  • Searchable bestiary with an optional knowledge gate
  • Full PC round-trip into the Arena and back — resources spent, wounds carried
Content, by the numbers

A content-complete SRD 5.1 layer under every world.

334
Monsters, with stat blocks
319
Spells
~590
Items · mundane, magic & poison
12
Classes + subclasses
15
Conditions, fully modeled
122k
Lines of hand-written code

Worlds you build in the Forge merge their own classes, spells, items, backgrounds and creatures on top of all of it.

Bring your own model

No lock-in. No account. Your key stays local.

The Connect your AI panel takes Anthropic, OpenAI, Google Gemini, or any local OpenAI-compatible server — Ollama, LM Studio, llama.cpp, no key needed. The model id is free text, so new models work the day they ship, and a Test button makes one tiny real call so a typo shows up as a sentence, never mid-game. No key at all? A scripted Offline Mode lets you look around.

◆ Anthropic ◆ OpenAI ◆ Google Gemini ◆ Local · Ollama / LM Studio
More from the table

The details that make it feel lived-in.

Get it

Two double-clicks and a key.

On Windows, run setup.bat once — it builds the environment and, if your PC has no suitable Python, quietly downloads a private one into the game folder. Then play.bat opens your browser to the start menu. Or from a clone:

# macOS / Linux / Windows
python -m venv .venv
pip install -e ".[web]"
oubliette-play                 # opens the browser game
View on GitHub → Read the feature guide
Oubliette · v0.9 · free & open source
